title: Quantum Fanout Gates in Constant Depth via Resonance Engineering
authors: Johannes Alexander Jaeger, Elias Zapusek, Florentin Reiter
abstract: We present a novel implementation of an n-qubit fanout gate using resonance engineering. Our proposed mechanism uses Jaynes-Cummings interactions between multiple qubits and a common harmonic oscillator to realize a fanout gate at the system-level. Our theoretical analysis establishes upper bounds on the gate error, demonstrating linear infidelity scaling in constant time -- a favorable trade-off compared to a conventional CNOT decomposition. To validate the performance of our scheme at large system sizes, we exploit permutation symmetry to reduce the simulation complexity from exponential to polynomial in the number of qubits, enabling simulation up to 100 qubits. The results of this numerical analysis are consistent with our theoretical findings and allow us to characterize the performance well. Our gate will enable faster stabilizer readouts and could provide polynomial speedups in many quantum algorithms.
summary: This paper proposes a new way to perform multi-qubit fanout gates in constant time using resonance engineering to block specific transitions. By optimizing the gate timing, the authors show that gate errors can be kept low even as the number of qubits increases up to 100. This could significantly speed up quantum algorithms like Grover's search and improve stabilizer measurements in error correction.
main_problem: The implementation of an n-qubit fanout gate in constant depth while minimizing gate infidelity caused by off-resonant excitations and error accumulation.
main_result: The authors propose a resonance engineering scheme that achieves a phase-flip fanout gate with linear infidelity scaling in constant time, demonstrating that errors can be effectively cancelled using an optimal timing condition.
method: The study uses analytical derivations of gate error bounds and numerical simulations in the Dicke basis, which reduces complexity from exponential to polynomial, allowing for simulations up to 100 qubits.
model_or_system: A collection of n qubits coupled to a common harmonic oscillator via Jaynes-Cummings type interactions, applicable to platforms like trapped ions and superconducting circuits.
key_observables: Gate fidelity, infidelity (1-F), and error probability.
important_parameters: The ratio of drive strengths (Omega_t/Omega_c), gate duration (t), and the number of target qubits (n).
main_assumptions: The scheme assumes the limit of weak probe drive relative to the strong blocking drive (Omega_t << Omega_c) and that the system can be described using a block-diagonal approximation in the Dicke basis.
figures_summary: Figure 1 shows the gate schematic; Figure 2 illustrates EIT-like energy level diagrams; Figure 3 depicts the gate mechanism steps; Figure 10 shows error probability vs. drive ratio; Figure 11 compares simulated fidelity to theoretical bounds.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the fanout gate problem, describes the physical mechanism using resonance engineering, provides theoretical error analysis and an optimal timing condition, presents large-scale numerical simulations using the Dicke basis, and discusses experimental feasibility and dissipation.
why_interesting: It presents a scalable method for many-qubit operations using collective coupling and resonance engineering, which is highly relevant for researchers working on controlling multi-qubit dynamics and error mitigation in cavity QED and trapped ion systems.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. Schematic representation of the fanout gate scheme. The qubit state is transferred to the harmonic oscillator using a Jaynes-Cummings drive (blue). This allows for the selective blocking of the Rabi drives (green) on the target qubits using a strong Jaynes-Cummings drive (red).

FIG. 2. An energy level diagram for the EIT system in- troduced in Eq. (3). The blue drive couples the states |2⟩ and |3⟩and is assumed to be far stronger than the green drive coupling the states |1⟩and |2⟩. We consider the system in the dressed state picture of the coupling drive Ωc, under which the states |2⟩and |3⟩hybridize to form dressed states |±⟩= |2⟩±|3⟩ √

FIG. 3. An energy level depiction of the steps of the gate mechanism. The states |a⟩|b⟩represent the tensor product of a qubit state |a⟩and HO state |b⟩. Panel (a) depicts the blue sideband π-pulse on the control qubit used to generate or remove an excitation. Panel (b) shows how resonance engi- neering is used to apply a phase to the |1⟩state on the target qubits conditioned on the number of excitations. A strong red sideband is applied to the target qubit, from an auxiliary state |e⟩to a second auxiliary state |f⟩. As this is a red side- band, it annihilates a phonon on the |e⟩state and therefore only couples to the 1-phonon state, while the 0-phonon state is dark. In the dressed state...

FIG. 4. Gate dynamics for excitation-dependent blocking of the probe drive for the case of multiple target qubits. (a) Couplings for three target qubits in the tensor-product ba- sis, with higher-order excitations induced by the probe drive (green) neglected, as resonance engineering suppresses popu- lation transfer out of the ground manifold. (b) The couplings expressed in terms of Dicke states, generalized for a state with m target qubits in the |1⟩state. The notation 01n1enefnf

FIG. 5. Fidelity of computational basis states with different number m of target qubits in the state |1⟩on the idle sub- space (control qubit |0⟩), as a function of time. Notice that due to Ω2 t ≪Ω2 c (1 and 49 in the example), the off-resonant excitations occur at approximately the same frequency.
